meta.title: CIRO Supervisor Exam — crash-course flashcards (master deck)
meta.design: Atomic pre-drill refresher. front = retrieval prompt; back ≤30 words; types: definition/threshold/list/distinction/process/trap. Keyed to syllabus.json element/outcome.
meta.qa: Every card citation-audited (contiguous verbatim source_quote, independently re-verified). Sufficiency gate: open-cards solve vs the question bank — 417 bank questions answered cards-only, 0 incorrect; all gaps patched or cross-deck-resolved. Cross-element near-duplicates retained deliberately (decks are studied standalone): FC-E1-0054/FC-E8-0032, FC-E3-0035/FC-E5-0044, FC-E4-0021/FC-E6-0021, FC-E4-0047/FC-E5-0052, FC-E4-0073/FC-E6-0011. See question-bank/qa-log.md.
meta.provenance: batches/ per-element files (incl. killed card FC-E9-0021)
meta.copyright_note: Original cards; quotes are public regulatory text. Nothing from CIRO's practice exam or CSI materials.

cards.front: What is the CSA and what does outcome 1.1 test about it?
cards.back: The umbrella of provincial/territorial securities and derivatives regulators — its jurisdiction, mandate and objectives, forms of legislation, registration requirements, enforcement powers.
cards.source_quote: "Understand the role and authority of the Canadian Securities Administrators (CSA) and provincial/territorial securities and derivatives regulators." — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 168

cards.front: Name the five forms of CSA legislation/policy instruments in syllabus 1.1.
cards.back: National Instruments (NI); Multilateral Instruments (MI); National policies; Staff Notices; Companion Policies (CP).
cards.source_quote: "- National Instruments (NI)" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 182; "- Multilateral Instruments (MI)" — ~line 186; "- National policies" — ~line 190; "- Staff Notices - Companion Policies (CP)" — ~lines 194-195

cards.front: Where does CIRO's regulatory authority come from?
cards.back: Recognition orders and delegated authorities from the securities regulators — CIRO is the recognized SRO, with its own jurisdiction, mandate and enforcement powers.
cards.source_quote: "Purpose of recognition orders and delegated authorities" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 210

cards.front: TRAP: which body issues the IDPC Rules and UMIR — the CSA or CIRO?
cards.back: CIRO. NIs/MIs/National policies/Staff Notices/Companion Policies are CSA instruments (1.1); IDPC Rules and UMIR are CIRO rule books (1.2).
cards.source_quote: "Investment Dealer and Partially Consolidated (IDPC) Rules • Universal Market Integrity Rules (UMIR)" — syllabus-raw.txt ~lines 220-221

cards.front: Who is involved when a firm registers as an Investment Dealer and its individuals seek approval?
cards.back: Both layers: the CSA's regulators and CIRO each have a role in dealer registration and individual approval.
cards.source_quote: "Understand the requirement for Investment Dealer registration and individual approval requirements and the role of CSA and CIRO in this process." — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 175

cards.front: List the four marketplace categories in syllabus 1.4.
cards.back: Exchanges; Alternative Trading Systems (ATS); Crypto-Asset Trading Platforms (CTPs); Foreign Organized Regulated Markets (FORM).
cards.source_quote: "• Exchanges" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 180; "• Alternative Trading Systems (ATS)" — ~line 184; "• Crypto-Asset Trading Platforms (CTPs)" — ~line 188; "• Foreign Organized Regulated Markets (FORM)" — ~line 192

cards.front: CDS vs CDCC — which is which?
cards.back: CDS = Canadian Depository for Securities Limited (securities); CDCC = Canadian Derivatives Clearing Corporation (derivatives). The two clearing agencies in syllabus 1.5.
cards.source_quote: "• Canadian Depository for Securities Limited (CDS) • Canadian Derivatives Clearing Corporation (CDCC)" — syllabus-raw.txt ~lines 201-202

cards.front: What four CIPF aspects does syllabus 1.6 test?
cards.back: Purpose and objective; funding requirements by Investment Dealers; governance structure; role in a dealer's bankruptcy/insolvency, including pooling of client assets.
cards.source_quote: "Role of CIPF in an Investment Dealer’s bankruptcy/insolvency, including pooling of client assets" — syllabus-raw.txt ~lines 216-217

cards.front: TRAP: CIPF protects clients against which kind of loss?
cards.back: Loss from an Investment Dealer's bankruptcy/insolvency (assets returned, pooled where needed) — never market losses, conduct losses, or unsuitable-advice losses.
cards.source_quote: "Role of CIPF in an Investment Dealer’s bankruptcy/insolvency, including pooling of client assets" — syllabus-raw.txt ~lines 216-217

cards.front: TRAP: match the 1.7 agency — suspicious-transaction analysis, federal financial-institution oversight, Ontario sectoral regulation, market-crime policing.
cards.back: FINTRAC = transaction analysis; OSFI = federal financial institutions; FSRA = Ontario; RCMP IMET = market-crime enforcement. Exam swaps these mandates.
cards.source_quote: "• Financial Transactions and Reports Analysis Centre of Canada (FINTRAC)" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 236; "• RCMP Integrated Market Enforcement Teams (IMET)" — ~line 232

cards.front: Which US securities/derivatives regulators must a Supervisor recognize (1.7)?
cards.back: Securities Exchange Commission (SEC); Financial Industry Regulatory Authority (FINRA); Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC); National Futures Association (NFA).
cards.source_quote: "US: Securities Exchange Commission (SEC), Financial Industry Regulatory Authority (FINRA), Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC), National Futures Association (NFA)" — syllabus-raw.txt ~lines 248-250

cards.front: The Bank Act's three purposes per syllabus 1.8 — and which other federal statute is paired with it?
cards.back: Ensure stability and integrity; protect consumers; promote competition. Paired with Bankruptcy and Insolvency Act Part XII — Bankruptcy of a Securities Firm.
cards.source_quote: "• Bank Act - Ensure stability and integrity - Protect consumers - Promote competition • Bankruptcy and Insolvency Act, Part XII – Bankruptcy of a Securities Firm" — syllabus-raw.txt ~lines 253-258

cards.front: List the eleven Criminal Code financial-crime categories in syllabus 1.9.
cards.back: Fraud; theft; theft by person holding POA; misappropriation under direction; false statement/pretenses; obtaining execution of security by fraud; forgery; uttering forged document; false prospectus; organized crime; illegal insider trading.
cards.source_quote: "• Fraud • Theft • Theft by person holding power of attorney • Misappropriation of money under direction • False statement or false pretenses • Obtaining execution of security by fraud • Forgery" — syllabus-raw.txt ~lines 261-267

cards.front: Name the three stages of money laundering tested under PCMLTFA (1.10).
cards.back: Placement, layering, integration — the syllabus tests 'stages of money laundering' as a PCMLTFA topic alongside compliance program requirements.
cards.source_quote: "• Stages of money laundering" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 283; "• Compliance program requirements" — ~line 287

cards.front: Besides ML stages and the compliance program, list the remaining PCMLTFA topics in 1.10.
cards.back: AML policies and procedures; client information and due diligence requirements; enterprise risk assessment and indicators; employee training; business relationship record-keeping.
cards.source_quote: "• Anti-money laundering policies and procedures" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 291; "• Client information and due diligence requirements" — ~line 295; "• Enterprise risk assessment and indicators" — ~line 299; "• Business relationship record-keeping" — ~line 307

cards.front: CASL's five purposes per syllabus 1.11?
cards.back: Reducing spam; enhancing privacy; promoting electronic commerce; deterring malicious activities; encouraging compliance and accountability.
cards.source_quote: "- Reducing spam" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 317; "- Enhancing privacy" — ~line 321; "- Promoting electronic commerce" — ~line 325; "- Deterring malicious activities - Encouraging compliance and accountability" — ~lines 329-330

cards.front: Corporation requirements, securities laws and applicable laws conflict. Which one governs?
cards.back: The most stringent: IDPC 1406(2) requires compliance with the most stringent of the Corporation requirements, securities laws or applicable laws.
cards.source_quote: "compliance with the most stringent of the Corporation requirements, securities laws or applicable laws is required" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 961

cards.front: An AP is the subject of a written client complaint. How fast must the AP report it, and to whom?
cards.back: Within two business days, to the Dealer Member (IDPC 3702(1)) — the AP's own clock, distinct from the dealer's reporting and acknowledgement timelines.
cards.source_quote: "An Approved Person must report to the Dealer Member any of the following matters within two business days" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 6149; "if the Approved Person is the subject of a written client complaint" — ~line 6154

cards.front: Deadline for the dealer's complaint acknowledgement letter to a retail complainant?
cards.back: Within five business days of receipt of the complaint (IDPC 3725(1)).
cards.source_quote: "The Dealer Member must send an acknowledgement letter to the complainant within five business days of receipt of a complaint." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 6411-6412

cards.front: Deadline for the dealer's substantive response to a retail complaint — and what if it can't be met?
cards.back: As soon as possible, not later than 90 days from receipt; if unable, inform the client (reasons, new estimate) and inform CIRO with reasons.
cards.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must respond to each client complaint as soon as possible and not later than 90 days from the date of receipt of the complaint" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 6454-6455

cards.front: Who is the designated complaints officer (DCO) and what must they have?
cards.back: The dealer-appointed individual with requisite experience and authority to oversee complaint handling and act as liaison with the Corporation (IDPC 3722(2)).
cards.source_quote: "The Dealer Member must appoint an individual to act as the designated complaints officer. The individual must have the requisite experience and authority to oversee the complaint handling" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 6370-6371

cards.front: TRAP: can a verbal client complaint trigger Part E retail complaint handling?
cards.back: Yes — a verbal expression of dissatisfaction alleging misconduct triggers Part E where a preliminary investigation indicates the allegation may have merit (3721(1)(ii)).
cards.source_quote: "a verbal expression of dissatisfaction with the Dealer Member or employee alleging misconduct where a preliminary investigation indicates that the allegation may have merit" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 6356-6357

cards.front: When must a dealer commence an internal investigation under IDPC 3706?
cards.back: If it appears the dealer or a current or former AP, while employed there, engaged in listed activities (theft, fraud, forgery, unauthorized trading, etc.) — any jurisdiction.
cards.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must conduct an internal investigation if it appears that the Dealer Member or a current or former Approved Person while employed by the Dealer Member engaged in any of the following" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 6262-6263

cards.front: Which client complaints must a dealer report to CIRO under 3703(2)(i)?
cards.back: All client complaints against the dealer or any current or former Approved Person — except service complaints (service issues not subject to securities laws).
cards.source_quote: "all client complaints, against the Dealer Member or any current or former Approved Person, except service complaints" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 6179

cards.front: What follows a failure to report under IDPC 3702/3703?
cards.back: CIRO may impose an administrative fee, or other penalties permitted under Corporation requirements, against the dealer or Approved Person (3704(1)).
cards.source_quote: "an administrative fee, or other penalties that are permitted under Corporation requirements, against the Dealer Member or Approved Person" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 6256-6257

cards.front: An AP with an open complaint moves to a new dealer. What is the AP's continuing duty?
cards.back: Continue to co-operate with the dealer where they were employed or acted as agent until the complaint is resolved (3727(1)); dealers must co-operate too.
cards.source_quote: "the Approved Person must continue to co-operate with the Dealer Member where they were employed or acted as an agent until the complaint has been resolved" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 6470-6471

cards.front: Arbitration vs ombudsman recourse: which binds the dealer?
cards.back: Arbitration: client request forces the dealer into binding arbitration (9502(4)). Ombudsman: recommendations are non-binding on each participant (9503(6)).
cards.source_quote: "If a client requests arbitration, the Dealer Member involved must submit to binding arbitration in" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 20211; "The ombudsman's recommendations are non-binding on each participant in the service." — ~line 20224

cards.front: The substantive response letter must describe which options for a dissatisfied client?
cards.back: Arbitration; litigation/civil action; submitting a complaint to the Corporation; the ombudsman service (within its time limit); any affiliate internal ombudsman (voluntary, with timing estimate).
cards.source_quote: "the Dealer Member’s response, including the availability of: (a) arbitration," — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 6442-6443; "(c) submitting a complaint to the Corporation," — ~line 6445

cards.front: TRAP: a client sues over the same allegations they complained about. Does Part E complaint handling still apply?
cards.back: No — any matter that is the subject of a civil action or arbitration is not a complaint for section 3721. Dealer reporting duties to CIRO continue.
cards.source_quote: "Any matter which is the subject of a civil action or arbitration is not considered to be a complaint" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 6365

cards.front: TRAP: CIRO's whistleblower service handles which kind of matters?
cards.back: Non-client account matters: misconduct or illegal activity within an organization, potential systemic wrongdoing, potential securities frauds. Client account matters go through complaint handling.
cards.source_quote: "Understand situations that may require CIRO whistleblower service for non-client account matters, including:" — syllabus-raw.txt ~lines 314-315; "• Potential systemic wrongdoing" — ~line 323

cards.front: Before an AP settles anything with a client, what must happen — and does it matter how the settlement arose?
cards.back: The dealer's written consent first — regardless of the settlement's form and regardless of whether it arose from a complaint or the AP's/dealer's own finding.
cards.source_quote: "An Approved Person must obtain the Dealer Member’s written consent before entering into any settlement agreement with a client, regardless of the form of the settlement" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 6299-6300

cards.front: TRAP: can a release bought with a settlement gag the client from going to regulators?
cards.back: No — a release may not impose confidentiality or similar restrictions preventing the client from initiating or continuing complaints to securities regulators, SROs or enforcement authorities (3711).
cards.source_quote: "A release entered into between a Dealer Member and a client may not impose confidentiality or" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 6310

cards.front: Maximum hearing-panel fine on an AP (Regulated Person other than a Dealer Member) under IDPC 8210?
cards.back: The greater of $5,000,000 per contravention and three times the profit made or loss avoided; plus reprimand, disgorgement, suspension, revocation, permanent bars available.
cards.source_quote: "a fine not exceeding the greater of: (a) $5,000,000 for each contravention, and (b) an amount equal to three times the profit made or loss avoided by the person" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 18293-18295

cards.front: State the IDPC 1402(1) standards of conduct.
cards.back: Observe high standards of ethics and conduct; act openly and fairly and in accordance with just and equitable principles of trade; no conduct unbecoming or detrimental to the public interest.
cards.source_quote: "act openly and fairly and in accordance with just and equitable principles of trade, and (ii) must not engage in any business conduct that is unbecoming or detrimental to the public" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 897-898

cards.front: Under 1402(2), which four kinds of business conduct MAY contravene the standards of conduct?
cards.back: Negligent conduct; failing a legal/regulatory/contractual or other obligation; unreasonable departure from expected standards; conduct likely to diminish investor confidence in market integrity.
cards.source_quote: "displays an unreasonable departure from standards that are expected to be observed by a Regulated Person, or (iv) is likely to diminish investor confidence in the integrity of securities or derivatives markets" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 904-905

cards.front: How does ethical behaviour relate to compliance per syllabus 1.17 — name the five qualities.
cards.back: Proper care; independent professional judgment; trustworthiness and integrity; honesty and fairness; professionalism.
cards.source_quote: "- Proper care - Independent professional judgment" — syllabus-raw.txt ~lines 344-345; "- Trustworthiness and integrity" — ~line 349; "- Honesty and fairness" — ~line 353; "- Professionalism" — ~line 361

cards.front: The four pillars of ethics and integrity in corporate governance (1.18)?
cards.back: Transparency; dealing with compensation conflicts; oversight and accountability; training and awareness.
cards.source_quote: "• Transparency" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 384; "• Dealing with compensation conflicts" — ~line 388; "• Oversight and accountability" — ~line 392; "• Training and awareness" — ~line 396

cards.front: Consequences and risks of unethical behaviour (1.19) — list all five.
cards.back: Legal consequences; reputational damage; regulatory scrutiny; employee morale and turnover; client confidence and turnover.
cards.source_quote: "• Legal consequences • Reputational damage • Regulatory scrutiny" — syllabus-raw.txt ~lines 401-403; "• Employee morale and turnover" — ~line 410; "• Client confidence and turnover" — ~line 414

cards.front: An AP identifies a material conflict between themselves and a client. First step?
cards.back: Promptly report that conflict of interest to the Dealer Member (3110(3)) — before any client disclosure or continued activity.
cards.source_quote: "Approved Person must promptly report that conflict of interest to the Dealer Member" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 3923

cards.front: Two conditions before an AP may trade/advise in connection with an identified material conflict (3111(3))?
cards.back: (1) The conflict has been addressed in the best interest of the client, and (2) the Dealer Member has consented to the AP proceeding with the activity.
cards.source_quote: "(i) the conflict has been addressed in the best interest of the client, and (ii) the Dealer Member has given the Approved Person its consent to proceed with the activity." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 3936-3937

cards.front: TRAP: does written conflict disclosure to the client discharge the dealer's/AP's conflict obligations?
cards.back: No — 3113(3): disclosure alone does not satisfy 3111(1)/3112(1). The conflict must be addressed in the client's best interest, or avoided if it cannot be.
cards.source_quote: "a Dealer Member and an Approved Person do not satisfy subsections 3111(1)" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 3963

cards.front: What does the 1.20 conflicts framework require for producing Supervisors?
cards.back: Applicable standards of care, ethics and independent supervision — a producing Supervisor's own client book cannot be self-reviewed; independent review applies.
cards.source_quote: "Applicable standards of care, ethics and independent supervision requirements for producing Supervisors" — syllabus-raw.txt ~lines 427-428

cards.front: Syllabus 1.20: the three conflict-identification categories, plus three further considerations?
cards.back: Categories: compensatory, managerial, ownership. Further: protection/proper use of corporate assets and opportunities; confidentiality of corporate/client/third-party information; fair dealing with all stakeholders.
cards.source_quote: "- Managerial - Ownership • Protection and proper use of corporate assets and opportunities • Confidentiality of corporate, client and third-party information • Fair dealing with securities holders, clients, suppliers, competitors and employees" — syllabus-raw.txt lines 438-443 ('Compensatory' verbatim at line 436, displaced by a PDF column artifact)

cards.front: Two threshold conditions any outside activity must satisfy (2554(1))?
cards.back: It is not contrary to securities laws or Corporation requirements, and does not bring the securities industry into disrepute. Fails either = prohibition, not approval.
cards.source_quote: "(i) is not contrary to securities laws or Corporation requirements, and (ii) does not bring the securities industry into disrepute." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 3038-3039

cards.front: Sequence for an AP to hold an outside activity under 2554(2)?
cards.back: AP informs the dealer; AP obtains the dealer's prior approval; dealer's P&Ps address continuous service to clients and potential conflicts; dealer notifies CIRO per NI 33-109.
cards.source_quote: "the Dealer Member notifies the Corporation of the outside activity within the time period and manner required by National Instrument 33-109" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 3048-3049

cards.front: Syllabus 1.21's analysis points for outside activities and positions of influence?
cards.back: Prohibition/approval/disclosure/action requirements; existing and reasonably foreseeable material conflicts of interest; written disclosure where applicable; reporting obligations where applicable.
cards.source_quote: "• Existing and reasonably foreseeable material conflicts of interest • Written disclosure, where applicable • Reporting obligations, where applicable" — syllabus-raw.txt ~lines 449-451

cards.front: State the IDPC 3115(1) baseline rule on personal financial dealings.
cards.back: An employee or Approved Person must not, directly or indirectly, engage in any personal financial dealings with clients — exemptions are narrow and conditional.
cards.source_quote: "An employee or Approved Person of a Dealer Member must not, directly or indirectly, engage in" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 3976

cards.front: Name the five personal-financial-dealing types listed in 3115(2).
cards.back: Accepting any consideration; settlement agreements without dealer approval; borrowing from clients; lending to clients; control or authority over a client's financial affairs.
cards.source_quote: "• Accepting any consideration" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 386; "• Settlement agreements" — ~line 390; "• Borrowing from clients" — ~line 394; "• Lending to clients" — ~line 398; "• Control or authority" — ~line 405

cards.front: When does a gift from a client escape the prohibited-consideration rule?
cards.back: When non-monetary, of minimal value, and infrequent — such that a reasonable person would not question whether it created a conflict (3115(2)(i)(a)(I)).
cards.source_quote: "Consideration that is non-monetary, of minimal value, and infrequent such that" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 3983

cards.front: Borrowing FROM a client: the two exemption routes under 3115(2)(iii)?
cards.back: (I) Client is a financial institution lending to the public, borrowing in its normal course; or (II) client is a Related Person (Income Tax Act), handled per dealer P&Ps.
cards.source_quote: "the client is a financial institution whose business includes lending money to" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 3999

cards.front: TRAP: lending TO a client — same exemptions as borrowing from one?
cards.back: Narrower: only the Related Person route exists (no financial-institution branch); plus, for RRs/IRs/PMs/APMs, prior written dealer disclosure-and-approval is required for both directions.
cards.source_quote: "Lending money, or providing a guarantee in relation to a loan of money, securities or" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4017

cards.front: TRAP: an AP quietly pays a client's account losses from personal funds. Allowed if no formal settlement is signed?
cards.back: No — paying for client account losses out of personal funds without the dealer's prior written consent is itself a prohibited personal financial dealing (3115(2)(ii)(b)).
cards.source_quote: "Paying for client account losses out of personal funds without the Dealer Member’s" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 3994

cards.front: Syllabus 1.23 containment processes for confidential/material non-public information?
cards.back: Information barriers and firewalls; grey and restricted list; cybersecurity — applied to those exposed (investment banking/corporate finance staff; issuer trading, research, insider relationships).
cards.source_quote: "• Information barriers and firewalls" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 430; "• Grey and restricted list" — ~line 434; "• Cybersecurity" — ~line 438

cards.front: Trading blackout for someone directly involved in preparing/approving a research report?
cards.back: No trading in the subject issuer's equity or equity-related securities from 30 days before until five days after issuance — absent prior written approval of a designated Executive.
cards.source_quote: "must not trade in equity securities or equity related securities of the subject issuer for a period beginning 30 days prior to and ending five days after the issuance of the research report" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 6039-6040

cards.front: Per syllabus 1.23, who is exposed to receiving confidential information?
cards.back: Investment banking and corporate finance staff; other staff exposed through, for example, trading by issuers, research departments, relationships with corporate insiders.
cards.source_quote: "- Investment banking and corporate finance staff" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 412; "• Trading by issuers • Research departments • Relationships with corporate insiders" — ~lines 423-425

cards.front: TRAP: a client pays an AP for services delivered through an APPROVED outside activity. Prohibited consideration?
cards.back: No — compensation for services through an approved outside activity is not 'consideration' under 3115(2)(i)(a).
cards.source_quote: "Compensation received from a client in exchange for services provided through" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 3988

cards.front: When MAY an AP hold control or authority over a client's financial affairs?
cards.back: Only if the client is a Related Person (Income Tax Act) and the control is addressed under the dealer's policies and procedures.
cards.source_quote: "the client is a Related Person as defined by the Income Tax Act (Canada) and" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4029

cards.front: What must the 5-day complaint acknowledgement letter include?
cards.back: Handler's name/title/contact; statement to contact the handler for status or new information; explanation of the internal process incl. the DCO's role; approved brochure + limitation-periods reference.
cards.source_quote: "the name, job title and full contact information of the individual at the Dealer Member" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 6414

cards.front: What compliance standard must a dealer's supervisory system deliver — and over whom?
cards.back: Reasonable assurance that ALL employees and Approved Persons comply with Corporation requirements and securities laws — not a guarantee, not retail-only.
cards.source_quote: "establish a supervisory system to supervise the activities of all its employees and Approved Persons that provides reasonable assurance they comply with Corporation requirements and securities laws." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 7883

cards.front: TRAP: supervision is whose fundamental responsibility, and WHO ensures an appropriate supervision system is in place?
cards.back: Supervision of all aspects of business and operations is the Dealer Member's fundamental responsibility; the board of directors — not the CFO or CIRO — ensures the system exists.
cards.source_quote: "The Dealer Member’s board of directors is responsible for ensuring that an appropriate supervision system is in place." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 7877

cards.front: At a minimum, supervisory policies and procedures must satisfy which four 3904(2) requirements?
cards.back: Specifically address supervision of employees/APs; provide reasonable assurance of compliance; be in writing; be amended within a reasonable time after rule/law changes.
cards.source_quote: "amend its policies and procedures relating to supervision within a reasonable time after changes in Corporation requirements, or securities laws are made." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 7900

cards.front: Name four 3904(3) communication duties owed to employees and Approved Persons.
cards.back: Obtain and record read-and-understood acknowledgements; introductory and continuing education on P&Ps; P&Ps on method/timing of compliance-notice distribution; promptly communicate P&P changes.
cards.source_quote: "obtain and record acknowledgements from all sales and supervisory employees and Approved Persons that they have read and understood the policies and procedures relevant" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 7906

cards.front: How often must the dealer's financial and operational P&Ps undergo a supervisory review?
cards.back: At least annually — and any deficiencies must be identified and corrected.
cards.source_quote: "a supervisory review of its financial and operational policies and procedures is completed at least annually and that any deficiencies are identified and corrected." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8058

cards.front: Why must Supervisors understand the dealer's books-and-records obligation (2.1 parameter)?
cards.back: The dealer's records provide an audit trail to support the Dealer Member's supervision of its business.
cards.source_quote: "A Dealer Member’s records provide an audit trail to support the Dealer Member’s supervision of its business" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 6526

cards.front: What must the written P&Ps required under 1404(2) establish, firm-wide?
cards.back: A system of controls and supervision sufficient to provide reasonable assurance the dealer, its employees and Approved Persons comply with Corporation requirements and securities laws.
cards.source_quote: "establish a system of controls and supervision sufficient to provide reasonable assurance the Dealer Member, its employees and Approved Persons comply with Corporation requirements and" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 937

cards.front: Before making a security or derivative available to clients, what three reasonable steps must the dealer take?
cards.back: Assess relevant aspects (structure, features, risks, initial/ongoing costs and their impact); approve it for availability; monitor it for significant changes.
cards.source_quote: "assess the relevant aspects of the securities or derivatives, including the securities’ or derivatives’ structure, features, risks, initial and ongoing costs and the impact of those costs," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5494

cards.front: Which accounts are exempt from the dealer's product due diligence obligation (3301)?
cards.back: Accounts maintained at a dealer that is a carrying broker for the account, or that only provides trade execution, clearing, settlement or custody services.
cards.source_quote: "Section 3301 does not apply in respect to an account maintained at a Dealer Member who is a carrying broker for that account or who only provides trade execution, clearing, settlement or custody services" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5510

cards.front: Product due diligence (3301) vs Know-Your-Product (3302) — who owes which duty?
cards.back: Dealer: assess/approve/monitor products made available. Approved Person: separately take steps to understand the product before transacting in or recommending it.
cards.source_quote: "recommend securities or derivatives to, a client unless the Approved Person takes steps to understand the securities or derivatives," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5503

cards.front: Who is responsible TO THE CORPORATION for the dealer's conduct and the supervision of its employees and Approved Persons?
cards.back: The Ultimate Designated Person (UDP).
cards.source_quote: "The Ultimate Designated Person is responsible to the Corporation for the conduct of the Dealer Member and the supervision of its employees and Approved Persons." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 7979

cards.front: What written reports must the CCO and CFO each provide, how often, and to whom?
cards.back: Each, at least annually, a written report to the board of directors: CCO on compliance generally; CFO on compliance with the financial Corporation requirements.
cards.source_quote: "At least annually, the Chief Financial Officer must provide a written report to the Dealer Member’s board of directors for the purpose of assessing compliance" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8041

cards.front: The board receives the CCO/CFO section 3915 reports. What two things must it then do?
cards.back: Review them to determine appropriate action to remedy identified compliance deficiencies, and ensure that action is taken (plus keep records of actions and monitoring).
cards.source_quote: "to determine the appropriate action to be taken to remedy any compliance deficiencies that are identified and must ensure that such action is taken." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8046

cards.front: Under Rule 1500, what must the dealer do — and document — for each significant area of risk?
cards.back: Assign responsibility for the risk area to an appropriate Executive, and document and maintain a list of Executives and the risk areas each manages.
cards.source_quote: "For each significant area of risk within a Dealer Member, the Dealer Member must assign responsibility to an appropriate Executive." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 979

cards.front: Who reviews and approves the policies and procedures for a given significant area of risk?
cards.back: The Executive responsible for that risk area — Executives are responsible for review and approval of P&Ps relating to their own significant area of risk.
cards.source_quote: "Executives are responsible for the review and approval of any policies and procedures relating to their significant area of risk." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 984

cards.front: An external auditor must report a material breach of Corporation requirements to CIRO in which three areas?
cards.back: Calculating the dealer's financial position; handling and custody of securities and precious metals bullion; maintaining adequate records.
cards.source_quote: "If during the regular conduct of an audit, the Dealer Member's auditor observes any material breach of Corporation requirements related to:" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 9121

cards.front: How do audits intersect with internal controls — what does the audit review, and who owns the controls?
cards.back: The substantive audit must review the accounting system and asset-safeguarding internal controls; Executives ensure adequate internal controls, review at least annually, and approve in writing.
cards.source_quote: "The audit of a Dealer Member requires a substantive approach and must include a review of the accounting system and the internal controls for safeguarding assets." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8932

cards.front: Name the three common areas of CIRO regulatory action against Approved Persons (syllabus 2.6).
cards.back: Violating securities and derivatives laws, regulations or CIRO requirements; aiding and abetting another in such violations; failing to satisfy applicable supervisory obligations.
cards.source_quote: "Violating securities and derivatives laws, regulations or CIRO requirements" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 578

cards.front: TRAP: a Supervisor who personally placed no improper trade argues only the RR can be disciplined. Why does this fail?
cards.back: Failing to satisfy applicable supervisory obligations is itself a common area of CIRO action — the supervision failure is independently sanctionable.
cards.source_quote: "Failing to satisfy applicable supervisory obligations" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 593

cards.front: An Approved Person resigns. For how long does CIRO's Rule 8100 investigative jurisdiction continue?
cards.back: Six years following the date they cease to hold the relevant capacities (e.g., employee or representative of a Dealer Member).
cards.source_quote: "A Regulated Person remains subject to Rule 8100 for six years following the date on which they cease to be:" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 18099

cards.front: Per syllabus 2.7, the compliance department's role spans which eight verbs?
cards.back: Identifying, assessing, advising on, acting on, communicating, monitoring, escalating and reporting on the dealer's compliance with regulatory requirements and its own P&Ps.
cards.source_quote: "Identifying, assessing, advising on, acting on, communicating, monitoring, escalating and reporting on the Investment Dealer’s compliance with regulatory requirements" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 604

cards.front: The CCO finds non-compliance creating a reasonable risk of harm to a client. Escalate to whom, and when?
cards.back: Report to the Ultimate Designated Person as soon as possible — same trigger for risk of harm to capital markets or a pattern of non-compliance.
cards.source_quote: "report to the Ultimate Designated Person as soon as possible if there is any indication that the Dealer Member or any individual acting on its behalf may be in non-compliance" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8003

cards.front: How do the CCO and CFO split the policies-and-procedures landscape?
cards.back: CFO: P&Ps relating to financial Corporation requirements. CCO: P&Ps assessing compliance other than those financial requirements.
cards.source_quote: "establish and maintain policies and procedures for the Dealer Member relating to financial Corporation requirements," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8014

cards.front: What must the section 1405 compliance monitoring system specifically address?
cards.back: Preventing and detecting violations, and procedures for reporting compliance-monitoring results to management.
cards.source_quote: "The compliance monitoring systems must specifically address preventing and detecting violations and include procedures for reporting the results of compliance monitoring to management." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 950

cards.front: TRAP: compliance monitors and flags — so is it primarily responsible for business conduct? Whose responsibility is it, and what do Rule 3900 standards give Supervisors?
cards.back: No — Registered Individuals are primarily responsible for compliance; Rule 3900's standards give Supervisors guidelines to monitor how those Registered Individuals handle their responsibilities.
cards.source_quote: "Registered Individuals1 are primarily responsible for compliance with business conduct" — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 31

cards.front: The IDPC definition of 'Supervisor' pairs which two attributes, granted by whom?
cards.back: Responsibility AND authority, given by the Dealer Member (and approved by the Corporation), to manage activities providing reasonable assurance of compliance.
cards.source_quote: "An individual given responsibility and authority by a Dealer Member, and approved by the Corporation, to manage the activities of the Dealer Member or the Dealer Member’s Approved Persons" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 826

cards.front: TRAP: a dealer names a Supervisor who can observe but not intervene. What requirement does this design break?
cards.back: Designated Supervisors and Executives must have the qualifications AND authority necessary to fully carry out the responsibilities assigned to them.
cards.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must designate Supervisors and Executives, with the qualifications and authority necessary to fully carry out the responsibilities assigned to them." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 7929

cards.front: A Supervisor detects activity outside proper conduct but lacks system permissions to act. Per the GN, what is an acceptable action?
cards.back: Escalating to a more senior Supervisor or Executive — Supervisors should have authority to take effective and timely remedial action.
cards.source_quote: "We consider escalating to a more senior Supervisor or Executive an acceptable action." — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 125

cards.front: What product proficiency must the dealer ensure in its Supervisors and Executives (3905(5))?
cards.back: Reasonable steps to ensure they are fully proficient and understand the products and services of those they supervise, to the degree necessary to properly supervise them.
cards.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must take reasonable steps to ensure all of its Supervisors and Executives are fully proficient and understand the products that employees and Approved Persons under their supervision trade in" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 7931

cards.front: Who supervises the Supervisors? What does 3905(6) require?
cards.back: The dealer must have procedures in place that ensure Supervisors are properly performing their supervisory functions.
cards.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must have procedures in place that ensure that Supervisors are properly performing their supervisory functions." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 7935

cards.front: Shared office premises P&Ps must give reasonable assurance of compliance — plus what client-facing outcome?
cards.back: That the client has a clear understanding of which entity they are dealing with.
cards.source_quote: "the client has a clear understanding of which entity they are dealing with." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8065

cards.front: The sole account-approval Supervisor goes on leave. What does Part B of Rule 3900 require instead of pausing approvals?
cards.back: Appoint one or more alternate Supervisors, as required, to assume the designated Supervisor's responsibility in their absence.
cards.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must appoint one or more alternate Supervisors as required, to the Supervisors designated in subsection 3925(2), to supervise the Dealer Member’s business" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8091

cards.front: Account supervision P&Ps: which three client-risk obligations and which record control must they specifically address?
cards.back: Identify clients high-risk to the dealer; clients high-risk for improper market activities; AML/ATF compliance; plus controls for accessing and amending client records.
cards.source_quote: "identify clients that present a high risk of conducting improper activities in the securities markets," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8100

cards.front: Per syllabus 2.8, a Supervisor's assigned supervisory responsibilities include which areas?
cards.back: Operation of account types; oversight of shared office premises; oversight of sales practices; method, timing and distribution of compliance-related notices.
cards.source_quote: "Operation of account types" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 528

cards.front: The Supervisor register (3908(1)) must record which three data points?
cards.back: Names of Supervisors; their supervisory responsibilities; the date each Supervisor was designated.
cards.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must maintain a record of the names of Supervisors, their supervisory responsibilities and the date each Supervisor was designated." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 7965

cards.front: Default retention period and form for dealer records, including supervision records?
cards.back: Minimum seven years from the date the record is created, in durable and accessible form — unless a different period is prescribed for that record type.
cards.source_quote: "in durable and accessible form, for a minimum of seven years from the date the record is created unless Corporation requirements or securities laws relating to the specific type" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 6721

cards.front: TRAP: supervision records are kept only at branch offices. What extra step does this trigger?
cards.back: The dealer must conduct periodic on-site reviews of branch office supervision and record keeping — branch storage does not relax head-office oversight.
cards.source_quote: "Where supervision records are kept at a branch office, the Dealer Member must conduct periodic on-site reviews of branch office supervision and record keeping." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 7971

cards.front: What evidence of supervisory reviews must the dealer keep, and for how long (3927(2))?
cards.back: Evidence of completed supervisory reviews, including details of inquiries about issues and their resolution, for the section 3803 period (seven-year minimum).
cards.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must record and keep evidence of completed supervisory reviews, including details of inquiries about issues and their resolution, for the period required in section 3803." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8129

cards.front: TRAP: what may a Supervisor delegate — and what can they never delegate?
cards.back: May delegate supervisory tasks and procedures; may NOT delegate the responsibility for their performance. The delegator retains responsibility.
cards.source_quote: "A Supervisor may delegate supervisory tasks and procedures, but not the responsibility for their performance." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 7950

cards.front: By which three routes can a delegate be qualified to perform assigned supervisory tasks?
cards.back: Registration, training or experience — the delegate need not be a designated Supervisor.
cards.source_quote: "A delegate must be qualified to perform the assigned tasks by virtue of registration, training or experience." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 7954

cards.front: When delegating, the Supervisor must take which three steps (3907(4))?
cards.back: Inform the delegate of tasks and expectations IN WRITING; ensure the delegate adequately performs them; establish reporting mechanisms for issues arising from the delegated tasks.
cards.source_quote: "inform the delegate of the tasks delegated to them and what is expected of them in the performance of the delegated tasks, in writing," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 7957

cards.front: What delegation record must the DEALER maintain, and what must the dealer tell the Supervisor up front?
cards.back: A record of the delegation terms plus the Supervisor's follow-up and review of delegated tasks; and inform the Supervisor of the specific functions that cannot be delegated.
cards.source_quote: "The Dealer Member must maintain a record of the terms of the delegation, as well as the Supervisor’s follow up and review of the delegated tasks." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 7961

cards.front: Minimum review frequency for the delegation process itself (syllabus 2.10)?
cards.back: Regular reviews — at minimum annual — with revisions to policies and procedures as necessary; plus regular reports from the delegate to the delegator.
cards.source_quote: "Regular (at minimum annual) reviews of the delegation process, with revisions to policies and procedures as necessary" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 523

cards.front: Examining a delegate's reviews tests two things — process compliance and what substantive standard?
cards.back: That the delegate follows applicable P&Ps AND recommends for approval only items that would have been approved by the delegator.
cards.source_quote: "Examination of the delegate’s reviews to ensure they are following applicable policies and procedures and to ensure that they are recommending items for approval that would have been approved by the delegator" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 632

cards.front: Besides examining delegate reviews, what escalation and trigger controls do the 2.10 delegation parameters require?
cards.back: Processes for escalating any significant issues to the delegator, and a list of factors that trigger a review by the delegator.
cards.source_quote: "Processes for escalating any significant issues to the delegator" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 630

cards.front: What is CIRO's published stance on automating supervisory tasks?
cards.back: Supervisory requirements are generally technology neutral; CIRO supports Dealers using automation to assist them with supervisory tasks.
cards.source_quote: "Generally, our supervisory requirements are technology neutral and we support Dealers using automation to assist them with supervisory tasks." — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 50

cards.front: TRAP: an automated system flags trades. Must the dealer investigate every flagged item — and what human role remains?
cards.back: No — exercise reasonable judgment in selecting items for further investigation. Automation screens; the Supervisor's role and responsibilities remain (some tasks cannot be automated).
cards.source_quote: "A Dealer is not required to enquire into or investigate every trade that meets the criteria in Rule 3900 or this Guidance, but should exercise reasonable judgment in selecting items" — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 38

cards.front: TRAP: continuous automated surveillance lets the dealer drop its designated account-approval Supervisor. True?
cards.back: False — the dealer must designate one or more Supervisors responsible for approving new accounts and for account-supervision procedures; automation cannot replace the designation.
cards.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must designate one or more Supervisors to be responsible for approving the opening of new accounts and for establishing and maintaining procedures relating to account supervision" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8086

cards.front: List the six syllabus 2.12 factors that may trigger a MANUAL review of an automated application.
cards.back: Applicant not an individual; trading authorization being used; applicant under the age of majority; derivatives strategies without appropriate knowledge/experience; insider; Politically Exposed Person (PEP).
cards.source_quote: "Applicant applying to use derivatives trading strategies and not having an appropriate level of knowledge and trading experience" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 588

cards.front: Besides manual-review triggers, what ongoing control do the 2.12 automation parameters require?
cards.back: Appropriate periodic testing and auditing to ensure the automated process is functioning as designed.
cards.source_quote: "Appropriate periodic testing and auditing to ensure automated process is functioning as designed" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 620

cards.front: How many Supervisors must a dealer appoint?
cards.back: As many as necessary to properly supervise its employees and Approved Persons, given the scope and complexity of the dealer's business — no fixed ratio or geographic formula.
cards.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must appoint as many Supervisors as necessary to properly supervise its employees and Approved Persons taking into account the scope and complexity of the Dealer Member's business." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 7923

cards.front: Per GN-3900-20-001, an effective supervisory structure considers which four business-model factors?
cards.back: Types of products traded and securities offered; type of trading engaged in; location of supervisory personnel and Registered Individuals; the different functions Supervisors are responsible for.
cards.source_quote: "types of products traded and securities ocered, type of trading the Dealer and its Registered Individuals engage in, location of the Dealer's Supervisory personnel and its Registered Individuals, and" — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 106

cards.front: A Supervisor detects activity outside just and equitable principles of trade but lacks authority to fix it. Acceptable action?
cards.back: Escalate to a more senior Supervisor or Executive — Supervisors should have authority to take effective and timely remedial action.
cards.source_quote: "Supervisors should have the authority to take ecective and timely remedial action when they detect activity that falls or appears to fall outside the bounds of proper conduct, just and equitable principles of trade or good business practice." — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 122

cards.front: A Supervisor is unqualified to supervise certain products their group trades. What may the dealer do?
cards.back: Divide supervision between two or more Supervisors, provided: communication mechanisms between them; the dealer keeps an overall view of the client's situation and activity; clear, complete assignment of responsibilities.
cards.source_quote: "If a Supervisor is unqualiﬁed to supervise trading activity in certain products by those under their supervision, a Dealer may divide the supervision between two or more Supervisors, provided:" — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 133

cards.front: Define 'internal controls' (IDPC Rule 1200) — and whose responsibility is their adequacy?
cards.back: Financial and operational policies/procedures established, maintained and applied by management for reasonable assurance of orderly, efficient business conduct. Executives ensure adequacy, as part of managing operations.
cards.source_quote: "The financial and operational policies and procedures established, maintained and applied by the Dealer Member's management to provide reasonable assurance of the orderly and efficient conduct of the Dealer Member's business." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 491

cards.front: Preventive controls vs detective controls — distinguish.
cards.back: Preventive: prevent, or minimize the chances of, fraud and error. Detective: discover, or increase the chances of finding, fraud or error, so prompt corrective action can be taken.
cards.source_quote: "Controls that discover, or increase the chances of finding, fraud or error, so the Dealer Member can take prompt corrective action." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 9237

cards.front: How often must Executives review internal controls, and what follows each review?
cards.back: At least annually (more frequently as necessary or as CIRO stipulates), reviewing adequacy and suitability; they must approve the internal controls in writing after each review.
cards.source_quote: "The Dealer Member's Executives must review a Dealer Member's internal controls for adequacy and suitability at least annually and more frequently as necessary or stipulated by Corporation" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 9257

cards.front: TRAP: setting up principal-agent arrangements — can an advisor's corporation act for the dealer, and what comes first?
cards.back: Only individuals (employees, including agents) may conduct the business — never a corporation; and before engaging any agents the dealer must enter a written agreement with the Corporation.
cards.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must not allow a corporation or other non-individual entity to conduct securities and derivatives related business on its behalf." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 1699

cards.front: TRAP: does the principal-agent (independent contractor) label reduce the dealer's obligations for the agent?
cards.back: No. The dealer-agent agreement must make the dealer liable to clients (and other third parties) for the agent's conduct as if they were an employee.
cards.source_quote: "liable to clients (and other third parties) for the agent's conduct as if they were an employee." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 1824

cards.front: TRAP: CIRO advises it is investigating an agent's alleged misconduct. Must the dealer wait for findings before acting on the agent's clients?
cards.back: No — the dealer may immediately, and without notice to the agent, assume responsibility for the client to the exclusion of the agent.
cards.source_quote: "the Dealer Member may immediately and without notice to the agent, assume responsibility for the client to the exclusion of the agent." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 1842

cards.front: At account opening, what two things must the dealer or agent disclose to clients about agent responsibilities?
cards.back: The list of securities/derivatives related business conducted by the agent for which the dealer is responsible; and that the dealer is not responsible for the agent's other business activities.
cards.source_quote: "the list of securities and derivatives related business conducted by the agent for which the Dealer Member is responsible, and (ii) that the Dealer Member is not responsible for any other business activity conducted by the agent." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 1739

cards.front: Order-execution-only (OEO) account model — the two defining features?
cards.back: The client is solely responsible for all investment decisions; the dealer provides no recommendation on any security or derivative. Written pre-opening disclosure plus positive client acknowledgement required.
cards.source_quote: "(i) the client is solely responsible for making all investment decisions, and (ii) the Dealer Member provides no recommendation to purchase, sell, hold or exchange any security" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 676

cards.front: An OEO services provider operating as a separate business unit must satisfy which structural separations?
cards.back: Separate letterhead, accounts and account documentation; its Registered Representatives and Investment Representatives may not work for any other business unit within the dealer.
cards.source_quote: "must have separate letterhead, accounts and account documentation, and its Registered Representatives and Investment Representatives may not work for any other business unit within the Dealer Member." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 1356

cards.front: An OEO client connects their own automated order software to the dealer's platform. Permitted?
cards.back: No — OEO clients must not use their own automated order system to generate orders to the dealer, or send orders on a pre-determined basis. That access belongs to DEA.
cards.source_quote: "use their own automated order system, as defined in securities laws, to generate orders to be sent to the Dealer Member or send orders to the Dealer Member on a pre‐determined basis, or" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4986

cards.front: What makes an account a direct electronic access (DEA) account — and why is it an entry risk?
cards.back: No suitability determination (limited exceptions); client has direct electronic access per NI 23-103; dealer gives no recommendations; UMIR/NI 23-103 compliance — client orders reach markets without intermediation.
cards.source_quote: "An account which is not subject to suitability determination (other than as required by clauses 3402(3)(i) and 3403(4)(i)) where: (i) the client has been provided with direct electronic access within the meaning of National Instrument 23-103," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 332

cards.front: Retail client vs institutional client — the definitional split?
cards.back: Retail = any client not institutional (residual). Institutional includes acceptable counterparties/institutions, regulated entities, non-individual registrants, and persons exceeding $10 million securities/bullion under administration or management.
cards.source_quote: "(v) a non-individual with total securities and precious metals bullion under administration or management exceeding $10 million," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 481

cards.front: Institutional supervision P&Ps must address detecting which improper or suspicious activities?
cards.back: Manipulative and deceptive activities; restricted-list trading (securities, and derivatives on restricted underlyings); front running by employee/proprietary accounts; restricted-transfer securities; exceeding derivative position/exercise limits.
cards.source_quote: "must specifically address detecting improper or suspicious account activity including: (i) manipulative and deceptive activities, (ii) trading in securities on the Dealer Member's restricted list," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8230

cards.front: Syllabus 3.5: list the account types whose supervisory risks must be analyzed (retail and institutional).
cards.back: Advisory; in-house managed; third-party managed; fee-based (non-managed)/wrap; discretionary; tax deferred; margin; Direct Electronic Access (DEA); DAP/COD/RAP settlement options.
cards.source_quote: "3.5 Analyze the supervisory risks associated with each of the following accounts for both retail and institutional clients as applicable. Consider:" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 714

cards.front: TRAP: may the designated Supervisor delegate the discretionary-account financial-performance reviews to a qualified delegate?
cards.back: No — performance of the 3970(1) and (2) reviews must not be delegated to any other person; stricter than the general delegation regime.
cards.source_quote: "The designated Supervisor responsible for discretionary accounts must not delegate the performance of the reviews required in subsections 3970(1) and 3970(2) to any other person." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8384

cards.front: Review cadence: discretionary accounts vs managed accounts?
cards.back: Discretionary: designated Supervisor reviews each account's financial performance at least monthly. Managed: each account reviewed quarterly (objectives pursued; handling complies with requirements).
cards.source_quote: "the designated Supervisor responsible for discretionary accounts must also review the financial performance of each discretionary account at least monthly." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8378

cards.front: KYC refresh cycle: managed/discretionary accounts vs other accounts?
cards.back: Baseline: review KYC information at least once every 36 months; managed and discretionary accounts at least once every 12 months.
cards.source_quote: "no less frequently than once every 36 months, except for a managed account and a discretionary account which must be reviewed no less frequently than once every 12 months." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4554

cards.front: TRAP: DAP/RAP institutional client wants no trade confirmations. What is the timing/form condition on the waiver?
cards.back: Prior to the trade, the client must have agreed in writing to waive confirmations — plus trade-matching, electronic audit trail, real-time access, and 85% compliant-trade-percentage conditions.
cards.source_quote: "(III) prior to the trade, the client has agreed in writing to waive receipt of trade confirmations from the Dealer Member," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 7722

cards.front: Which extra KYC step does a margin account trigger?
cards.back: Establish the creditworthiness of the client whenever the dealer is financing the client's acquisition of a security.
cards.source_quote: "(iv) establish the creditworthiness of the client if the Dealer Member is financing the client's acquisition of a security." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4433

cards.front: Third-party managed accounts: what conflict-of-interest condition must a sub-advisor meet?
cards.back: Be subject to legislation/regulations with conflict provisions at least equivalent to section 3280, or have agreed with the dealer to comply with section 3280.
cards.source_quote: "subject to legislation or regulations containing conflict of interest provisions at least equivalent to those set out in section 3280 or has entered into an agreement with the Dealer Member that it will comply with section 3280." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5444

cards.front: May an Associate Portfolio Manager send portfolio advice to managed account clients unilaterally?
cards.back: No — a Portfolio Manager at the dealer must approve the advice before the APM provides it; the APM is under required direct supervision.
cards.source_quote: "including a prohibition on the Associate Portfolio Manager providing advice unless the advice has been approved by a Portfolio Manager at the Dealer Member prior to the Associate Portfolio Manager providing the advice." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8420

cards.front: Syllabus 3.6: the three basic types of securities whose supervisory risks must be analyzed?
cards.back: Equities; mutual funds and ETFs; fixed income products.
cards.source_quote: "3.6 Analyze the supervisory risks associated with each of the following basic types of securities: • Equities" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 666

cards.front: Equity-risk detection: which 3945(2) retail items target concentration and portfolio decay?
cards.back: Undue concentration of securities, derivatives or precious metals bullion in a single account or across accounts; deterioration of the quality of client holdings in an account.
cards.source_quote: "(ii) undue concentration of securities, derivatives or precious metals bullion in a single account or across accounts, (iii) excessive trading," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8146

cards.front: Syllabus 3.7: the four complex security/product types to analyze?
cards.back: Leveraged and inverse ETFs; structured products; asset-backed securities; specialized products (e.g. cryptocurrency).
cards.source_quote: "3.7 Analyze the supervisory risks associated with each of the following complex types of securities/products:" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 681

cards.front: TRAP: do leveraged and inverse ETFs sit with basic 'mutual funds and ETFs' in the syllabus?
cards.back: No. Conventional mutual funds/ETFs are basic securities (3.6); leveraged and inverse ETFs are listed as complex products (3.7) requiring distinct supervisory risk analysis.
cards.source_quote: "• Leveraged and inverse ETFs" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 686

cards.front: Complex products: the dealer's three-step product due diligence, and the AP's matching duty?
cards.back: Dealer: assess relevant aspects (structure, features, risks, costs and their impact); approve products made available; monitor for significant changes. AP: take steps to understand before recommending.
cards.source_quote: "(ii) approve the securities or derivatives to be made available to clients, and (iii) monitor the securities or derivatives for significant changes." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5496

cards.front: Syllabus 3.8: list the derivative types whose supervisory risks must be analyzed.
cards.back: Options; futures contract options; futures; forwards; swaps; CFDs; other similar derivatives as described by CIRO.
cards.source_quote: "3.8 Analyze the supervisory risks associated with each of the following types of derivatives:" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 703

cards.front: Derivatives supervision: which two designated Supervisors, and what if coverage gaps arise?
cards.back: One for options contracts/similar derivatives; one for futures, forwards, CFDs, futures contract options/similar. Appoint alternate Supervisor(s) if necessary for continuous supervision.
cards.source_quote: "appoint a designated Supervisor to supervise its activities that involve futures contracts, forward contracts, contracts for difference, futures contract options or similar derivatives." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8281

cards.front: Options and futures exposures: which two derivative-specific items must transaction reviews detect?
cards.back: Exposures arising out of uncovered option positions; exposures to delivery obligations through holding futures, forwards and similar derivatives into the delivery month.
cards.source_quote: "(x) exposures arising out of uncovered option positions, and (xi) exposures to delivery obligations through the holding of futures contracts, forward contracts and similar derivatives into the delivery month." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8359

cards.front: TRAP: retail CFD guardrails — float limit and shareholder features?
cards.back: Prevent retail positions exceeding 0.5% of a publicly traded issuer's float intra-day/short-term; prohibit retail CFDs conferring delivery rights/obligations or shareholder rights such as voting.
cards.source_quote: "prevent a retail client from holding contracts for difference or similar derivatives positions representing more than 0.5% of the float of a reporting issuer" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8331

cards.front: OEO accounts offering OTC derivatives (e.g. CFDs) to retail clients: what profitability disclosure goes with the risk disclosure statement?
cards.back: The percentage of such accounts that were profitable for each of the four most recent quarters — realized outcomes, not projections.
cards.source_quote: "a disclosure of the percentage of such accounts that were profitable for each of the four most recent quarters." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5298

cards.front: TRAP: a dealer with commission AND fee-based accounts screens monthly reviews by commission levels only. Why deficient?
cards.back: Commission screens (churning risk) make fee-based activity invisible (reverse-churning/neglect risk): accounts cannot be selected solely on commissions; a fee-based selection procedure is also needed.
cards.source_quote: "ocers commission and fee-based accounts cannot select accounts for review solely based on commission levels, they should also have a procedure for selecting fee- based accounts." — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 186

cards.front: Fee-based (no-commission) retail accounts: what must the dealer's supervision P&Ps do?
cards.back: Specifically address supervising retail accounts where no trade commission is charged: cover account activity review requirements and use criteria other than commission levels.
cards.source_quote: "specifically address supervising retail client accounts where a commission is not charged for trades placed by or for a client, such as fee based accounts." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8175

cards.front: Two compensation prohibitions: OEO employees and managed-account handlers?
cards.back: No trade commissions to employees for transactions executed in OEO accounts; no compensating persons authorized to deal with managed accounts on the value/volume of account transactions.
cards.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must not compensate employees by giving them trade commissions for transactions executed in order execution only accounts." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 1363

cards.front: TRAP: which compensation pair belongs to outcome 3.10 — and which to 3.9?
cards.back: 3.10: negotiated flat fees and referral fees (Understand). 3.9: commission-based and fee-based structures (Analyze). Exam distractors swap the pairs.
cards.source_quote: "3.10 Understand the supervisory risks associated with negotiated flat fees and referral fees." — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 770

cards.front: When may a dealer charge a managed account client a negotiated fee tied to transaction volume/value or contingent on profit/performance?
cards.back: Only if the client has provided a written agreement setting out the manner in which such fees may be charged — otherwise prohibited.
cards.source_quote: "unless the client has provided the Dealer Member with a written agreement which sets out the manner in which the fees may be charged based on volume or value of transactions or contingent upon profit or performance." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5476

cards.front: TRAP: must every swap transaction generate a standard trade confirmation?
cards.back: No — an industry-standard agreement acceptable to the Corporation may set out the terms for the client to receive swap confirmations and their key terms.
cards.source_quote: "the agreement sets out the terms for a client to receive a confirmation of a swap transaction and the key terms of the confirmation." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 7749

cards.front: By when must a designated Supervisor approve each new account?
cards.back: No later than one business day after completing the initial trade for the account.
cards.source_quote: "A designated Supervisor must approve each new account no later than one business day after completing the initial trade for the account." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 4643-4644

cards.front: How does interim account approval work?
cards.back: An alternative procedure may approve new accounts on an interim basis — designated Supervisor's final approval due no later than one business day after the initial trade.
cards.source_quote: "A Dealer Member may use an alternative procedure to approve new accounts on an interim basis, provided the designated Supervisor provides final approval no later than one business day after the initial trade." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 4645-4647

cards.front: Supervisor does NOT approve the account after the initial trade. Consequence?
cards.back: Restrict the account to only liquidating trades, transfers out, paying out funds or delivering securities to the client — until final approval.
cards.source_quote: "the Dealer Member must restrict the account to only liquidating trades, transfers out, paying out funds or delivering securities to the client" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 4649-4650

cards.front: Required documents are still missing after account opening. By when must the dealer take specific action?
cards.back: Within 25 business days of opening the account, unless a shorter period is prescribed.
cards.source_quote: "take specific action to obtain required documents that have not been received within 25 business days of opening the account, unless a shorter period is prescribed" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 4631-4632

cards.front: TRAP: 1 business day vs 25 business days — which governs what?
cards.back: 1 business day after initial trade: Supervisor account approval (3214(3)). 25 business days: specific action on outstanding documents (3213(2)(iii)).
cards.source_quote: "A designated Supervisor must approve each new account no later than one business day after completing the initial trade" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 4643-4644

cards.front: What two conditions govern assigning an account number to a new account?
cards.back: Full and accurate client name and address known to the dealer; complete account application received no later than the following business day.
cards.source_quote: "may only assign an account number to a new account if the full and accurate name and address of the client who holds the account is known" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 4638-4639

cards.front: Opening an account for an employee of ANOTHER Dealer Member requires what?
cards.back: Written approval from the other Dealer Member, and designation of the account as a non-client account.
cards.source_quote: "the Dealer Member must obtain written approval from the other Dealer Member, and must designate the account as non-client account" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 4652-4654

cards.front: What conflict-of-interest control applies to verifying significant changes to client information?
cards.back: Policies and procedures independent of the Registered Representative, Portfolio Manager or Associate Portfolio Manager — the record-controller must not self-verify.
cards.source_quote: "have policies and procedures independent of the Registered Representative, Portfolio Manager or Associate Portfolio Manager for verifying significant changes to client information" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 4633-4635

cards.front: TRAP: reusing a current application copy to record changes — 36 months or 12 months?
cards.back: 36 months for ordinary accounts; 12 months for managed or discretionary account applications. Changes initialled by RR/PM/APM and their Supervisor.
cards.source_quote: "If the client's managed account or discretionary account application was approved within the past 12 months, the Dealer Member may use a copy" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 4677-4678

cards.front: List the seven branches of the institutional client definition.
cards.back: Acceptable counterparty; acceptable institution; regulated entity; non-individual registrant; non-individual >$10M securities/bullion; individual >$10M who requests and consents; hedger who requests and consents (qualifying hedging accounts).
cards.source_quote: "a non-individual with total securities and precious metals bullion under administration or management exceeding $10 million" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 481-483

cards.front: TRAP: which institutional-client branches require the client's request and consent?
cards.back: The individual >$10M and the hedger branches. A non-individual >$10M qualifies without request or consent.
cards.source_quote: "a hedger who requests and consents to being classified as an institutional client for accounts with qualifying hedging activities and hedge positions" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 488-490

cards.front: Define 'hedger' (Rule 1201).
cards.back: A non-individual exposed to risks as a necessary part of its business activities, hedging via derivatives whose underlying interest is the same as or materially related to the risk.
cards.source_quote: "A non-individual that: (i) is exposed to one or more risks as a necessary part of its business activities" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 430-432

cards.front: What must be verified and recorded for hedger/institutional classification?
cards.back: Verify the client qualifies as institutional (3212(2)); records must demonstrate the determination undertaken to classify a client as a hedger and as institutional (3804).
cards.source_quote: "demonstrate determination undertaken to classify a client as a hedger and as an institutional client" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 6771-6772

cards.front: An institutional client subject to suitability is judged NOT capable of independently evaluating risk. The dealer must:
cards.back: Take steps to ensure the institutional client fully understands the investment product, including the potential risks.
cards.source_quote: "the Dealer Member must take steps to ensure that the institutional client fully understands the investment product, including the potential risks" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5634-5635

cards.front: Account appropriateness: what two determinations, made when?
cards.back: Before opening, on a reasonable basis putting the person's interest first: the action is appropriate; the scope of products, services and account relationships is appropriate.
cards.source_quote: "Before a Dealer Member opens an account for a person, the Dealer Member must determine, on a reasonable basis and putting the person's interest first" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 4573-4574

cards.front: TRAP: which accounts are exempt from the SCOPE-of-products limb of appropriateness, 3211(1)(ii)?
cards.back: Order execution only accounts and direct electronic access accounts. They remain subject to 3211(1)(i) — the basic appropriateness determination.
cards.source_quote: "Clause 3211(1)(ii) does not apply in respect to: (i) an order execution only account, or (ii) a direct electronic access account." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 4582-4584

cards.front: Which accounts escape the ENTIRE 3211(1) appropriateness determination?
cards.back: Carrying-broker / pure execution-clearing-settlement-custody accounts; accounts held by a Dealer Member, regulated entity, exempt market dealer, portfolio manager, bank, trust company or insurance company.
cards.source_quote: "an account held by a Dealer Member, regulated entity, exempt market dealer, portfolio manager, bank, trust company or insurance company" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 4590-4591

cards.front: Name the four enumerated client account records in section 3210.
cards.back: Documentation supporting identity verification; documentation supporting the account appropriateness assessment; KYC information collected per Corporation requirements; the client's account application.
cards.source_quote: "documentation supporting the conclusion that the client's identity has been verified, (ii) documentation supporting the account appropriateness assessment" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 4564-4566

cards.front: Fee condition for relying on an affiliate's documentation?
cards.back: Account service fees/charges, and charges for making, disposing and holding investment products, must be the same or lower as at the affiliate firm.
cards.source_quote: "the following fees and charges associated with the account offering and investment products and services are the same or lower as those at the affiliate firm" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 4604-4605

cards.front: TRAP: must Relationship Disclosure be given to institutional clients?
